quote: the small but very real possibility that 2025 could see the worst cyberattack in history, perhaps catalyzed in some fashion by generative AI
claim: There is a real possibility that 2025 could see the worst cyberattack in history, potentially catalyzed by generative AI

quote: Depressingly, despite all of the apparent progress in the last few years, much of what we raised in 2015 and re-emphasized in 2019 remains unsolved.
claim: The core problems of commonsense reasoning in AI identified in 2015 and 2019 remain unsolved despite recent progress in AI

quote: no single technique would be adequate in itself, and that the solution to the problem would probably require new techniques, as well as better integration between those that already existed
claim: Solving commonsense reasoning will require new techniques and better integration of existing approaches, not any single technique alone

quote: Rosie the Robot is not doing our housework, and that is in part because the kind of physical reasoning a household robot would need to do can't be done purely by running simulations
claim: Physical simulation alone cannot achieve the commonsense physical reasoning required for general-purpose household robots

quote: In Elon Musk's recent Optimus demonstrations, all or most of the commonsense required was apparently outsourced to human teleoperators.
claim: Tesla's Optimus robot demonstrations relied on human teleoperators for commonsense reasoning rather than autonomous AI

quote: more recent models no longer makes the same specific mistakes that GPT-3 did, but still lack a robust understanding of the real external world
claim: Recent LLMs have fixed specific mistakes of earlier models but still lack robust understanding of the external physical world

quote: because it does not understand how objects are embedded in space and interact in space or, indeed, what spatial relationships actually mean, it often makes very basic mistakes
claim: OpenAI's o1 does not understand how objects are embedded in and interact in space, leading to basic spatial reasoning mistakes

quote: There is not much reason to think that a robot could actually use such video streams in a practical sense to reliably navigate, act, and plan in the real world.
claim: Video generation systems like Sora cannot produce outputs that robots could use for reliable real-world navigation, action, and planning

quote: fundamentally its videos are more like visual collages than the renderings of game engines, which is why they sometimes have a surreal or magic trick quality
claim: Sora's videos are visual collages rather than physics-based renderings, which is why they exhibit surreal physical behavior

quote: they will not work well until major progress is made in common sense, which itself may require wholesale revisions in how AI is approached
claim: AI agents will not work well until major progress is made on commonsense reasoning, which may require wholesale revisions in AI approaches

quote: Hoping that reliable commonsense will simply "emerge" from large databases of text and video has proven unrealistic, again and again.
claim: Reliable commonsense reasoning will not emerge from simply training on large databases of text and video

quote: one important component of physical and conceptual understanding is reasoning about entities and their properties, and current approaches have consistently fallen short on this
claim: Current AI approaches consistently fall short on reasoning about entities and their properties, a key component of physical and conceptual understanding

quote: it is only once AI researchers grapple directly with the challenging problems inherent in commonsense reasoning about entities that interact in space and persist in time that the field of general-purpose AI will finally begin to mature
claim: General-purpose AI will only mature once researchers directly tackle commonsense reasoning about entities that interact in space and persist in time

quote: the best was Claude 3.5, at 24%
claim: The best LLM agent (Claude 3.5) achieved only 24% on TheAgentCompany benchmark of real-world employee tasks

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: I do not share his confidence.
claim: Marcus does not believe OpenAI knows how to build AGI as traditionally understood

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: LLMs, like their predecessors generalize well to similar items but struggle with reliability in unfamiliar regimes, even minor variations sometimes break them
claim: LLMs generalize well to similar items but struggle with reliability in unfamiliar regimes, with even minor variations sometimes breaking them

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: 30% decrement by o1 on math problems with minor variations eg on variable names
claim: OpenAI's o1 shows a 30% performance decrement on math problems with minor variations such as changed variable names

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: Commonsense reasoning is still dodgy, as it has long been
claim: Commonsense reasoning in current AI systems remains unreliable

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: In o1's best performances cases, there may have been heavy data augmentation that is not possible in more open-ended domains.
claim: o1's best benchmark performances may rely on heavy data augmentation that is not feasible in open-ended domains

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: the current idea, test-time "scaling", is very expensive, and will likely prove unreliable out of domains where augmentation is adequate, in which case it will get comparatively little commercial traction
claim: Test-time scaling (inference compute) is very expensive and will likely prove unreliable outside domains where data augmentation is adequate, limiting its commercial viability

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: Even o1 is losing money; o3 isn't even available, and perhaps cannot be run at a profit.
claim: OpenAI's o1 is losing money and o3 may not be able to run at a profit

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: the presentation of o3 was problematic, and no clean test of out-of-domain generalization was provided
claim: OpenAI's presentation of o3 was problematic and provided no clean test of out-of-domain generalization

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: Many leading figures in the field have acknowledged that we may have reached a period of diminishing returns of pure LLM scaling, much as I anticipated in 2022.
claim: Pure LLM scaling has reached a period of diminishing returns, as Marcus predicted in 2022

trigger: Sam Altman blog post claiming AGI confidence
quote: the lack of distinct, accessible, reliable database-style records leads to hallucinations
claim: The lack of distinct, accessible, reliable database-style records in LLMs is a root cause of hallucinations

quote: the definition of AGI, while not perfect, is reasonably clear — and has been reasonably clear — for a long time
claim: The definition of AGI has been reasonably clear for a long time

quote: a shorthand for any intelligence ... that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence
claim: AGI should be defined as intelligence that is flexible, general, resourceful, and reliable, comparable to or beyond human intelligence

quote: One could imagine, for example, replacing every single job one job at a time, each with its own separate domain-specific systems. That would be impressive, but would not reflect the General in Artificial General Intelligence.
claim: Replacing jobs one at a time with domain-specific systems would not constitute AGI even if economically significant

quote: Current models trained on next-token prediction have remarkable abilities and remarkable weaknesses. The scope of those abilities and weaknesses of the current systems is not well understood.
claim: Current next-token-prediction models have remarkable abilities and remarkable weaknesses whose scope is not well understood

quote: there is zero justification for claiming that the current technology has achieved general intelligence
claim: There is no justification for claiming current AI technology has achieved general intelligence

quote: it's becoming increasingly clear that they are struggling in building a model worthy of the GPT-5 name
claim: OpenAI is struggling to build a model worthy of the GPT-5 name

quote: We might see GPT-5 this year, but given bottlenecks on data and inherent limits in LLMs we may well not.
claim: GPT-5 may not ship in 2025 due to data bottlenecks and inherent LLM limitations

quote: The hypothesis I have been floating since 2022 that scaling data alone would not be enough for robust AI has become increasingly prevalent
claim: Scaling data alone is not sufficient for achieving robust AI

quote: 500,000 humanoid robots in three years, which seems to very unlikely
claim: Elon Musk's prediction of 500,000 humanoid robots in three years is very unlikely to be achieved

quote: I will stand by my own predictions that we won't see AGI in 2025
claim: AGI will not arrive in 2025

quote: I won't be at all surprised if don't even see anything worthy of the GPT-5 name
claim: It would not be surprising if nothing worthy of the GPT-5 name ships in 2025

quote: LLMs don't meet that; as the world has discovered, reliability is not their strong suit
claim: LLMs do not meet the definition of AGI because reliability is not their strong suit

quote: LLMs lack the ability to reliably generalize to novel circumstances
claim: LLMs lack the ability to reliably generalize to novel circumstances

quote: the inability of LLMs to do basic fact checking and sanity checking speak to their lack of resourcefulness
claim: LLMs are unable to perform basic fact-checking and sanity-checking of their own outputs

quote: Counting on AI that is shallow and not reliable, it should also be said, could cause huge problems. We can never guarantee that broad but shallow AI will be safe, or that it will be human-compatible
claim: Relying on broad but shallow AI that is unreliable could cause huge problems because its safety and human-compatibility cannot be guaranteed

quote: LLMs often use the words without a deep understanding what those words mean
claim: LLMs use words without deep understanding of what those words mean

quote: A system answering a river-crossing problem should not answer by superficial analogy to the words in similar problems found in a giant scrape of the internet, but rather by reasoning through concepts about boats, rivers, people, and so on.
claim: AI systems should solve problems by reasoning through concepts rather than by superficial word-level analogy to training data

quote: Moving past broad but shallow AI and into a new and hopefully safer and more reliable regime should be a major research priority for the human species.
claim: Moving past broad-but-shallow AI toward safer and more reliable AI should be a major research priority

quote: GenAI answers are frequently superficial; they invent things ("hallucinations" or what I would prefer to call confabulations"), they fail to sanity check their own work, and they regularly make boneheaded errors in reasoning, mathematics and so on.
claim: Generative AI answers are frequently superficial, produce hallucinations, fail to self-check, and regularly make errors in reasoning and mathematics

quote: I do genuinely think we will all have our own AI agents, and companies will have armies of them. And they will be worth trillions, since eventually (no time soon) they will do a huge fraction of all human knowledge work, and maybe physical labor too.
claim: AI agents will eventually do a huge fraction of all human knowledge work and possibly physical labor, and will be worth trillions

quote: But not this year (or next, or the one after that, and probably not this decade, except in narrow use cases). All that we will have this year are demos.
claim: Reliable general-purpose AI agents will not arrive in 2025, 2026, 2027, or probably this decade, except in narrow use cases

quote: And those demos won't work all that reliably.
claim: AI agent demos in 2025 will not work reliably

quote: you can't build reliable agents unless you can solve the hallucination problem, and there is still no principled trustworthy answer to that problem
claim: Reliable AI agents cannot be built until the hallucination problem is solved, and no principled solution to hallucination exists yet

quote: you can't build agents unless you have common sense, sound reasoning, a tight grounding in reality, and systems that reliably do you what you ask of them, with enough theory of mind to infer your intentions
claim: Reliable AI agents require commonsense, sound reasoning, grounding in reality, reliable instruction-following, and theory of mind

quote: Easily jailbroken AI agents that excel at mimicry but lack the capacity and imperative to evaluate the consequences of their actions are going to cause massive harm.
claim: Easily jailbroken AI agents that lack the ability to evaluate consequences of their actions will cause massive harm

quote: "Mainlining them" into the veins of society, as the UK proposes to do, is a huge mistake.
claim: The UK's proposal to deeply integrate AI agents into society is a huge mistake given their security vulnerabilities

trigger: Sam Altman walking back AGI claims
quote: stands by his prediction that we will not see AGI in 2025
claim: AGI will not be achieved in 2025

quote: The entirely speculative conjecture that LLMs or something else OpenAI figures out how to be build will be enormously profitable. So far the infrastructure field wide (250B, perhaps) has enormously outweighed total revenue, perhaps 50:1.
claim: AI infrastructure spending (~$250B industry-wide) has enormously outweighed total AI revenue, perhaps by a ratio of 50:1

quote: The entirely speculative conjecture that any profits will actually do much to help the American people, as opposed to just enriching those who own that infrastructure.
claim: It is speculative that AI infrastructure profits will help the American people broadly rather than just enriching infrastructure owners

quote: if the data centers work towards better AI, many others will lose their jobs. Net effect is entirely unclear.
claim: If AI data centers produce better AI, the net employment effect is entirely unclear because job losses may offset jobs created

quote: The equally speculative conjecture that the U.S. will long term have an advantage over China if the U.S. builds the hypothesized infrastructure, as if China isn't trying to build the same. On LLMs China is already catching up.
claim: US AI infrastructure investment will not guarantee a long-term advantage over China because China is catching up on LLMs

quote: The race for "AI Supremacy" is over, at least for now, and the U.S. didn't win.
claim: The race for AI supremacy is over for now, and the US did not win

quote: realistically, we are quickly converging on a tie — with some style points to China, for doing so much without hundreds of thousands of Nvidia H100s
claim: The US and China are converging on a tie in AI capabilities, with China earning credit for achieving results with far fewer high-end chips

quote: There is almost no moat left whatsoever; new technical leads very short lived, measured in months or even weeks, not years.
claim: There is almost no moat in LLMs; technical leads are measured in months or weeks, not years

quote: GPT-5 has yet to arrive, and when OpenAI introduces new models, competitors quickly catch up. There is no obvious moat whatsoever.
claim: OpenAI has no obvious competitive moat because competitors quickly catch up whenever OpenAI introduces new models

quote: I have said it before, and I will say it again—OpenAI may well become the WeWork of AI.
claim: OpenAI may become the WeWork of AI (i.e., a high-profile collapse after massive overspending and hype)

quote: Nvidia has basically been getting rich selling (exceptionally well-designed) shovels in the midst of gold rush, but may suddenly face a world in which people suddenly require far fewer shovels.
claim: Nvidia may face a sharp decline in demand as LLM training becomes more efficient and requires fewer high-end chips

quote: the DeepSeek results suggest that it is possible to get by without NVidia's top-of-the-line chips
claim: DeepSeek's results demonstrate that state-of-the-art LLM training can be done without Nvidia's top-of-the-line chips

quote: the CHIPS Act, which was designed to slow China in the AI race, may turn out to be one of the worst backfires in history
claim: The CHIPS Act export controls may turn out to be one of the worst policy backfires in history by spurring China's efficiency innovations

quote: We accidentally upped their technical game.
claim: US export controls accidentally improved China's technical capabilities by forcing them to innovate on efficiency

quote: revenue (order of $5B) has been modest, compared to outlays that have been much greater; no actual killer app (relative to the costs) has emerged
claim: OpenAI's revenue (~$5B) is modest compared to its much larger outlays, and no killer app justifying the costs has emerged

quote: Advances will be more incremental than before and quickly matched. GPT-5 or a similarly impressive model will come eventually
claim: Future AI advances will be more incremental than before and will be quickly matched by competitors

quote: Models will continue to get more efficient and less expensive, but hallucinations and reliability problems will persist.
claim: AI models will continue to get more efficient and cheaper, but hallucination and reliability problems will persist

quote: neither country will achieve AGI by the end of 2027
claim: Neither the US nor China will achieve AGI by the end of 2027

quote: Racing endlessly around LLMs will sap resources that might go into developing more original ideas.
claim: Continued investment focused on LLMs will divert resources from developing more original and potentially superior AI approaches

quote: The only hope for the US to regain a clear lead is for a government agency, a US company, or an academic lab to think outside the LLM box.
claim: The US can only regain a clear AI lead by innovating beyond LLMs, not by building more LLM infrastructure

quote: LLMs are too opaque, too unwieldy, and too difficult to debug and verify. The answer lies elsewhere
claim: LLMs are too opaque, unwieldy, and difficult to debug and verify to be the path to AGI; the answer lies elsewhere

quote: The race to AGI will be won not by the country with the most chips but by the one that best fosters true innovation.
claim: The race to AGI will be won by the country that best fosters true innovation, not the one with the most chips

quote: if the race to LLMs continues to be unregulated in the US, and LLMs remain detached from reality, rushed development cycles and intense global competition might well exacerbate risks of misinformation, biased outputs, privacy breaches, and misuse by malicious actors
claim: Unregulated LLM competition combined with unreliable AI will exacerbate risks of misinformation, bias, privacy breaches, and misuse

quote: a rumor I heard at Davos, which fits with some earlier reporting from the Wall Street Journal and another well-placed source I read recently, is that OpenAI is struggling to build GPT-5, focusing instead on the user interface
claim: OpenAI is struggling to build GPT-5 and is instead focusing on user interface improvements to find a non-technical advantage

quote: Nvidia could soon take a serious hit
claim: Nvidia's stock could soon take a serious hit due to DeepSeek's demonstration that LLMs can be trained more efficiently

quote: DeepSeek r1 is not smarter than earlier models, just trained more cheaply
claim: DeepSeek R1 is not smarter than prior frontier models; it is trained more cheaply but not more capable

quote: It doesn't solve hallucinations or problems with reliability.
claim: DeepSeek R1 does not solve the hallucination or reliability problems of LLMs

quote: It is still expensive to operate ("inference"), especially if you want to make it "think" longer like o3.
claim: DeepSeek R1 is still expensive to operate at inference time, especially with extended reasoning like o3

quote: The biggest threat isn't to Nvidia (people will still need GPUs, albeit fewer higher end ones), but rather to OpenAI and Anthropic (because price wars will undercut their hopes of making a profit).
claim: DeepSeek's biggest threat is to OpenAI and Anthropic's profitability via price wars, not to Nvidia's business

quote: DeepSeek is an economic revolution, and geopolitical wake-up call, but that doesn't directly bring us any closer to AGI.
claim: DeepSeek represents an economic revolution and geopolitical wake-up call but does not bring us closer to AGI

quote: we don't actual KNOW this; we humans are flexible, general, resourceful thinkers but it is not at all clear that we actually need the equivalent of millions of chips to achieve what we do
claim: It is not clear that building human-level AI actually requires millions of chips, since humans achieve flexible general intelligence with only ~20 watts

quote: The real lesson of DeepSeek is that we can still make vast improvements in efficency. When all is said and done, with the right software, we might be able to build intelligence with hundreds or even dozens of GPUs.
claim: With the right software, it may be possible to build AI intelligence using only hundreds or dozens of GPUs rather than millions

quote: 2026-2027 are made up dates. I hereby offer Dario a $100,000 bet that we will not have AI that is 'smarter than almost all human beings' by the end of 2027
claim: AI will not be smarter than almost all human beings by the end of 2027

quote: so far those controls have not been terribly successful; and may even have had paradoxical effects
claim: US AI chip export controls have not been successful and may have had paradoxical effects by spurring Chinese innovation

quote: the LLM race is an enormously expensive effort to win a race that can no longer be won
claim: The LLM race is an enormously expensive effort to win a race that can no longer be won by any single country

quote: The country that does its best to develop a new tech that is more reliable and more difficult to replicate will win.
claim: The country that develops new AI technology that is more reliable and harder to replicate than LLMs will win the AI race

quote: what we really need are better ideas, and the oxygen for innovative startups to breathe
claim: Winning the AI race requires better ideas and support for innovative startups, not just more chips for incumbents

quote: a company that made its name regurgitating and recombining sliced-up bits of intellectual property in statistically probable ways without due compensation is now threatened by … another company apparently doing the same, at lower cost
claim: OpenAI built its business by using others' intellectual property without due compensation and is now being threatened by DeepSeek doing the same to them

quote: Deep Research, because it works faster and expands the reach of what can be automatically generated, is going to make that problem worse.
claim: OpenAI's Deep Research will worsen the problem of AI-generated misinformation and garbage content on the internet

quote: Scammers will use it, for example, to write "medical reports" that are half-baked (in order to sell ads on the webistes that report such "research").
claim: Scammers will use Deep Research to generate half-baked medical reports for ad-driven websites

quote: the larger problem may be naive people who believe that the outputs are legit, or who use it as a shortcut for writing journal articles, missing the errors it produces
claim: The larger problem with Deep Research is naive users who will believe its outputs are legitimate or use it as a shortcut for journal articles while missing its errors

quote: virtually everything that Deep Research produces will pass a LGTM test ( "it 'looks good to me'"); people will assume it's legit, even when it is isn't.
claim: Deep Research outputs will almost always pass a superficial review (LGTM test) even when they contain errors

quote: Students will quickly learn that every term paper it writes gets an A.
claim: Students will quickly learn that Deep Research can write term papers that receive high grades

quote: Scientific researchers will start to use it, too (as Unutmaz recommended). And inevitably errors will seep in to the scientific literature, often undetected.
claim: Scientific researchers will use Deep Research and errors will inevitably seep into the scientific literature, often undetected

quote: The output of DeepResearch will inevitably feed further models; we will wind up with more of this
claim: Deep Research output will feed back into training data for future models, accelerating model collapse

quote: paper mill factories (there is already such a thing, designed to game citation counts, "an illegitimate, yet lucrative business" that is growing) will massively ramp up production, choking the peer review process and saturating the market with hard-to-find, hard-to-rectify errors
claim: Paper mill factories will massively ramp up production using Deep Research, choking peer review and saturating scientific literature with errors

quote: When — not if — Deep Research begins to flood the medical zone with shite, we are all in trouble.
claim: Deep Research will flood the medical information space with unreliable content, creating serious public health risks

quote: GPT-3 is the king of pastiche.
claim: GPT-3 operates by creating pastiche -- imitating the style and content of human text without understanding it

quote: it is mimic that knows not whereof it speaks
claim: LLMs are mimics that do not understand the content they produce

quote: Knowledge is in part about specific properties of particular entities. GPT's mimicry draws on vast troves of human text that, for example, often put together subjects [England] with predicates [won 5 Eurovision contests].
claim: LLMs draw on statistical associations between subjects and predicates in training text but lose track of precise entity-property bindings

quote: GPT's heavy use of a technique called embeddings makes it really good at substituting synonyms and more broadly related phrases, but the same tendency towards substitution often lead it astray.
claim: LLMs' embedding-based approach makes them good at synonym substitution but the same mechanism leads them to make inappropriate substitutions

quote: It never fully masters abstract relationship.
claim: LLMs never fully master abstract relationships between entities

quote: When GPT gets things right, it is often combining bits that don't belong together, but not quite in random ways, but rather in ways where there is some overlap in some aspect or another.
claim: When LLMs produce correct-seeming output, they are combining fragments that share some surface-level overlap but may not actually belong together

quote: At least for now, it still takes a human to know which plausible bits actually belong together.
claim: Humans are still required to judge which plausible-seeming LLM outputs are actually correct

quote: ChatGPT still seems likes pretty much the same mix of brilliance and stupidity that I wrote about, more than two years ago
claim: ChatGPT in early 2025 still exhibits the same fundamental mix of brilliance and stupidity as two years ago

quote: failed, multiple times, to properly count to 50
claim: ChatGPT fails multiple times at basic tasks like counting to 50 and listing all US states

quote: reported that the letter h could be a vowel, at least when it appeared in the word Northwest
claim: ChatGPT incorrectly classified the letter 'h' as a vowel when appearing in the word 'Northwest'

quote: issued numerous corrections that were wrong, never acknowledging uncertainty until after its errors were called out
claim: ChatGPT issues confident corrections that are themselves wrong and only acknowledges uncertainty after errors are pointed out

quote: if AI can't do Kapoor's expense reports or my simple tables, is AGI really imminent? Who is kidding whom?
claim: AI's inability to reliably handle expense reports and simple table generation demonstrates AGI is not imminent
